children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

5.I.1	The Department of Peace Operations is responsible for supporting the maintenance of international peace and security by providing political and executive direction to peace operations within its purview in accordance with and by the authority derived from the principles and purposes of the Charter of the United Nations. The mandate derives from the priorities established in relevant General Assembly resolutions and decisions, including the Pact for the Future (resolution 79/1) and resolution 72/262 C, the annual comprehensive reviews of the entire question of peacekeeping operations in all their aspects and the annual consideration of cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations. The objectives of the programme are also conceived by taking into account Security Council mandates. The Department provides integrated strategic, political, operational and management advice, direction and support on military, police, mine action and other relevant issues to all operations under its responsibility in order to ensure the effective implementation of their mandates. Through its efforts to improve peacekeeping, enhance its performance and adapt to contemporary risks and challenges, the Department will continue to help conflict-affected countries on their path back to sustainable peace. The work of the Department is critical to continuously ensuring the relevance and effectiveness of United Nations peacekeeping, which remains a vital instrument for international peace and security.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

5.I.2	The strategy of the Department continues to be guided by all relevant Security Council mandates. The Department will continue to prioritize the full implementation of the peace and security reform objectives. The Action for Peacekeeping initiative and its implementation strategy will continue to guide the programme, in line with the commitments set out in the Declaration of Shared Commitments on United Nations Peacekeeping Operations, taking into account the views expressed by Member States. The Department will work to implement all actions in the Pact for the Future that are relevant to peacekeeping operations, including any mandates and reform efforts pursuant to the review on the future of all forms of United Nations peace operations. The areas of focus continue to be:

(a)	To enhance political strategies and advance lasting political solutions, including by developing country and regional strategies, through the integrated approach of the peace and security pillar, to develop proposals for the consideration of the Security Council on the sequencing and prioritization of mandates and to reflect Action for Peacekeeping priorities in reporting;

(b)	To implement the women and peace and security agenda across all peacekeeping functions, including through gender-sensitive conflict analysis, the distribution of guidance to ensure women’s full, equal and meaningful participation in peace processes and the systematization of reporting on women and peace and security outcomes;

(c)	To strengthen the protection provided by peacekeeping operations, including by maintaining the protection of civilians, where mandated, as one of the core objectives of the relevant peacekeeping operations, updating and disseminating the policy and training standards on the protection of civilians in United Nations peacekeeping, integrating strategic approaches to protection into the strategic thinking and action of missions and conducting context-specific training, including predeployment training, on the protection of civilians, on the protection of children and on preventing and responding to conflict-related sexual violence;

(d)	To support effective performance and accountability of all mission components through the ongoing implementation of the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System, as part of the integrated peacekeeping performance and accountability framework, to evaluate missions and strengthen their impact using data and analysis, as well as through enhanced implementation of the human rights due diligence policy on United Nations support to non-United Nations security forces;

(e)	To strengthen the impact of peacekeeping on sustaining peace, including by implementing joint transition and mobilization strategies and coordinating closely with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, including the Peacebuilding Support Office, as well as other United Nations entities and external partners;

(f)	To improve the safety and security of peacekeepers, including by implementing the related action and training plans aimed at integrating new technologies and security protocols tailored to mission-specific environments, including specialized training on the use of technology to promote information integrity and counter emerging threats, such as misinformation, disinformation and hate speech, and improvised explosive devices, which should be implemented with transparency and in consultation with Member States, as appropriate;

(g)	To improve peacekeeping partnerships by strengthening United Nations-African Union cooperation on political efforts, including through joint field visits and briefings, as well as through standardized and tailored training, capacity-building and the light coordination mechanism, which will strengthen efforts to identify training requirements and match capacity-building providers with new and emerging troop-contributing countries in need of support;
(h)	To strengthen the conduct of peacekeeping operations and personnel and ensure that all types of misconduct are addressed in accordance with the zero-tolerance approach.

5.I.3	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, partnerships with peace and security actors, in particular regional organizations, and with humanitarian and development actors, including international financial institutions, will remain essential to strengthening the overall impact of international efforts in peacekeeping contexts. The Department will maintain its strategic and operational partnerships with the African Union and other regional and subregional organizations in Africa, as well as with the European Union. The Department will continue to cooperate closely with other regional organizations, including the League of Arab States, the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, the Collective Security Treaty Organization, the Association of Southeast Asian Nations and the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, on issues related to policy, information-sharing and training, among others.

5.I.4	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, the Department will continue to review and refine the implementation of the reform of the peace and security pillar and ensure coherence, working with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs with a view to better linking cross-cutting functions across the two Departments, including with regard to training, innovation, partnerships and information integrity. The Department will work closely with the Department of Management Strategy, Policy and Compliance and the Department of Operational Support in assisting peacekeeping operations. The Department will maintain strong coordination with the reformed management and development pillars.

5.I.5	The Department will continue to maintain its strong relations with other United Nations agencies, funds and programmes by chairing or participating in a range of inter-agency groups and processes, including the United Nations-World Bank Working Group, the Inter-Agency Working Group on Disarmament, Demobilization and Reintegration, the Inter-Agency Security Sector Reform Task Force, the Inter-Agency Coordination Group on Mine Action and the Inter-Agency Task Force on Policing. Other cross-agency cooperation will be undertaken through the global focal point arrangement for the rule of law and the Peacebuilding Strategy Group.

5.I.6	With regard to the external factors, the Department considered applicable risks identified in its risk register and, accordingly, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Member States continue to support the advancement of the Action for Peacekeeping initiative and its implementation strategy, as well as reform efforts pursuant to the review on the future of all forms of United Nations peace operations;
(b)	The Security Council reaches consensus on clear, focused and achievable mandates for peacekeeping operations and Member States support their implementation;
(c)	Peacekeeping partners and other entities sustain the common political strategies supported by peacekeeping operations;
(d)	Availability of capabilities required for deployment of peacekeeping operations and successful implementation of their mandates.

5.I.7	The Department integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. Through the newly established joint Gender Unit of the Department of Peace Operations and the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, the Department will enhance implementation of the women and peace and security agenda through guidance on women’s full and meaningful participation in peace and political processes, gender-sensitive analysis and the mainstreaming of a gender perspective into planning, budgeting, implementation and reporting, and through the promotion of the increased representation of women uniformed personnel. Strengthened collaboration through the joint Gender Unit of the two Departments, and through close partnership with the United Nations Entity for Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN-Women) and other relevant entities, will also enable more coherent support to missions and country teams.

5.I.8	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, the Department will support Secretariat-wide efforts on disability inclusion, including through the implementation of a joint Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs-Department of Peace Operations disability inclusion action plan.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

5.I.9	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated planned deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026. In addition, in collaboration with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, the Department has advanced efforts to further consolidate the single, shared regional political-operational structure. The two Departments have also consolidated functions related to peacebuilding, disarmament, demobilization and reintegration, security sector reform, and justice and corrections, as outlined under the strategy of subprogramme 3. This consolidation will strengthen the coherence and effectiveness of peacebuilding and peace support efforts across peacekeeping operations, special political missions and non-mission settings. The unified structure will provide more coherent support to Member States, from prevention through transition, ensuring that security gains are reinforced by institution-building and development. In addition, to further enhance the implementation of the women and peace and security agenda, the Department will enhance collaboration with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, as outlined in para 5.I.7 above. Further coordination of policy, training and innovation functions between the two Departments will also be undertaken, as outlined in paragraph 5.I.4 above.

5.I.10	The Department will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals emerging from the review on the future of all forms of United Nations peace operations requested in General Assembly resolution 79/1.

children.text: Evaluation activities

5.I.13	The Department will continue to implement the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System in all United Nations peacekeeping operations, as part of the integrated peacekeeping performance and accountability framework. The System remains a key component of this framework, enabling the Department and missions to assess overall mission performance, using data and evidence to strengthen mandate implementation.
5.I.14	Three evaluations to be conducted by the Department on its programme activities are planned for 2027.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Operations
Objective

5.I.15	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure the effective and efficient implementation of all Security Council mandates to plan, establish and adjust peace operations within the purview of the Department, as well as the effective implementation of relevant General Assembly resolutions, in order to maintain international peace and security.
Strategy
5.I.16	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Improve mission- and context-specific analysis and reporting on peacekeeping to the Security Council, the General Assembly and other intergovernmental bodies and troop- and police-contributing countries;

(b)	Ensure that integrated planning processes are completed according to Security Council substantive and time requirements, with an emphasis on supporting transitions between peacekeeping operations and follow-up United Nations presences, where required;

(c)	Incorporate regional aspects into country- and mission-specific strategies as part of the emphasis on a regional approach to peace, security and stability further to the reform of the peace and security pillar.
5.I.17	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Better information on peacekeeping operations being available to the General Assembly, the Security Council and other intergovernmental bodies to inform their decision-making;
(b)	Better planning for mandate delivery by peacekeeping operations and for transitions;
(c)	More coherent and impactful United Nations engagement and support in matters of peace and security provided to countries and the regions in which they are situated.
Programme performance in 2025
Progress in the implementation of the 2019 Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation in the Central African Republic

5.I.18	With the subprogramme’s support, the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in the Central African Republic (MINUSCA) continued to implement its mandate in a comprehensive and strategic manner, contributing to further progress in the implementation of the 2019 Political Agreement for Peace and Reconciliation in the Central African Republic. In 2025, several signatory armed groups returned to the peace process and entered into the national disarmament, demobilization and reintegration programme. In this regard, MINUSCA supported 15 disarmament operations in 11 locations, resulting in the disarmament and demobilization of 1,263 ex-combatants, including 21 women, and the collection of 854 weapons, 135 grenades and 40,241 rounds of ammunition. In addition, with support from the subprogramme, MINUSCA provided extensive good offices, logistical, security and technical support for the organization of combined presidential, legislative, regional and municipal elections, which were held in a generally peaceful environment on 28 December 2025 across all prefectures. Municipal elections, which had not been held since 1988, were a significant outstanding provision of the Political Agreement.

children.text: Result 2: strengthened peacebuilding in Abyei through the Joint Programme of the Resident Coordinators in the Sudan and South Sudan in Abyei – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

5.I.22	The subprogramme’s work, through the United Nations Joint Programme in Abyei, contributed to United Nations country team members maintaining their engagement in Abyei, resulting in tangible peacebuilding and resilience gains there, which met the planned target. These included: (a) expanded access to water through the installation of new water systems and training for water user committees on water management and governance; (b) strengthened livelihoods through seed and tool distribution for 1,400 farmers, vocational education for 275 young people, the establishment of three women’s cooperatives comprising 150 women, and the construction of a fish and vegetable market; (c) improved health services through mobile and outreach health clinics, which delivered essential medical kits, cholera kits and other medical supplies, with around 40,000 people benefiting from primary healthcare consultations; and (d) enhanced intercommunal engagement through various dialogues and workshops to promote trust, cooperation and joint economic projects and counter hate speech.

children.text: Result 3: enhanced disarmament, demobilization, repatriation, resettlement and reintegration of foreign combatants in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.I.24	The United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO), with the subprogramme’s support, continues to implement its strategic priorities as outlined in Security Council resolution 2808 (2025) to contribute to: (a) the protection of civilians in its area of deployment; (b) the achievement of the objectives set by the Council in its resolution 2773 (2025); and (c) the stabilization and strengthening of State institutions in the Democratic Republic of the Congo. As part of these efforts, MONUSCO intensified its support to disarmament, demobilization, repatriation, resettlement and reintegration processes and mechanisms, including by contributing to the disarmament, resettlement and repatriation of Forces démocratiques de libération du Rwanda (FDLR) combatants as part of the FDLR neutralization plan. Since January 2025, MONUSCO has facilitated the repatriation of 207 ex-combatants and their dependants from Goma to Rwanda.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.I.25	The lesson for the subprogramme was the need to ensure greater involvement of local communities in disarmament, demobilization, repatriation, resettlement and reintegration efforts, taking into consideration that FDLR combatants are often embedded within local communities, making disarmament and repatriation efforts more challenging. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will strengthen community awareness-raising initiatives to enhance awareness of such efforts and promote the greater involvement and cooperation of local communities in facilitating disarmament and repatriation efforts.

children.text: Subprogramme 2 – Military
Objective
5.I.28	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to maintain international peace and security through the effective performance of military components of peace operations.
Strategy
5.I.29	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Provide briefings and analysis to Member States on the military aspects of new or anticipated developments, and crisis and security situations;

(b)	Strengthen partnerships with regional organizations by supporting them in the development of plans for peace operations, generate and deploy appropriate military units, conduct operational assessments and advisory visits, and prepare new troop-contributing countries for future participation in peace operations;

(c)	Train and enhance communication with senior military leaders, update military standards for the military component to validate the operational readiness of military units prior to deployment, and conduct in-mission performance evaluations, predeployment/pre-rotation visits and military capability studies;
(d)	Facilitate the participation of women in peace operations, at all levels, through engagement with troop-contributing countries;

(e)	Further refine the military performance evaluation system, including implementation of the military performance evaluation tool, further develop an evaluator training mechanism and initiate and deploy a performance evaluation process for individual uniformed personnel;

(f)	Further refine and develop military doctrine, policies and training materials to support performance evaluation, enhance operational readiness and performance, and improve the safety and security of peacekeepers.
5.I.30	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved reporting to the Security Council, the General Assembly, intergovernmental bodies and troop-contributing countries;
(b)	Rapid deployment and establishment of or adjustments to peacekeeping operations in response to Security Council mandates and related decisions;
(c)	Deployment of better-tailored, better-equipped and more relevant military components to peacekeeping operations;
(d)	Improved performance and increased safety of military components in peacekeeping operations;
(e)	A safe and secure environment in the host country, with a view to enhancing the safety of civilians and the delivery of humanitarian services.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened preparedness for the transition and withdrawal of United Nations peacekeeping missions

5.I.31	The Special Committee on Peacekeeping Operations, in its 2024 report (A/78/19), stressed the importance of all peacekeeping missions having realistic and adaptable contingency plans that can address various scenarios and ensure the security of personnel. It also requested the Secretariat to ensure that every peacekeeping mission has appropriate contingency plans, including withdrawal plans, which implement relevant lessons learned and take into consideration the changing security environment. In response, the subprogramme continued to develop the exit concept of operations, which provides operational direction to the heads of military components for the development of detailed plans and directives related to the withdrawal of military personnel. These exit concepts of operations are designed to ensure the safe, secure and orderly extraction of peacekeepers and outline procedures for withdrawal under permissive, semi-permissive and non-permissive conditions in deteriorating security environments. In 2025, the subprogramme finalized three exit concepts of operations, for the United Nations Peacekeeping Force in Cyprus, MONUSCO and the United Nations Interim Security Force for Abyei, building on progress achieved in 2024, when the exit concept of operations for the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL) was finalized.

children.text: Result 3: peace operations strengthen monitoring, analysis and response to misinformation, disinformation and hate speech – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.I.37	Peace operations continue to operate in increasingly complex environments characterized by both physical attacks and hostile activities in the information domain. In MONUSCO, for example, the proliferation of misinformation, disinformation and hate speech has affected the ability of the Mission to implement its mandate and exacerbated risks to United Nations personnel and assets. In response, the subprogramme has supported efforts to enhance monitoring and analysis of the information environment and to strengthen coordination between operational and public information components in MONUSCO. The Department, in collaboration with the Office of Information and Communications Technology, developed the Monitoring and Analysis of the Information Environment (MAIE) platform, which uses advanced analytics to track online discourse and identify disinformation and misinformation in peacekeeping settings, as well as Unite Wave, which analyses local FM radio broadcasts in several local languages.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.I.38	The lesson for the subprogramme was that improvements in military operations alone are insufficient to mitigate reputational and perception-based risks that may affect the safety and security of United Nations uniformed personnel. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support the development of a more integrated approach to strengthening information integrity and addressing misinformation, disinformation and hate speech directed against peacekeeping operations and personnel. This will include enhanced monitoring and analysis of the information environment through the Monitoring and Analysis of the Information Environment platform; improved coordination between uniformed components and public information capacities; and the production of verified, field-based communication products aligned with operational realities, with a view to reducing anti-United Nations sentiment and mitigating associated risks to personnel, assets and mandate implementation.

children.text: Subprogramme 3 – Rule of law and security institutions
Objective

5.I.40	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to strengthen the rule of law and security sector governance to contribute to the maintenance of international peace and security in countries where peacekeeping operations and special political missions deploy, or where otherwise authorized, or requested by Member States, in full compliance with the Charter of the United Nations.
Strategy

5.I.41	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will provide advisory and operational support to peacekeeping operations and special political missions, as well as resident coordinators, and where otherwise authorized or requested by Member States. Specifically, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Assist host States in the reform, restructuring and development of their police and other law enforcement institutions, provide operational support and, in rare circumstances, assume interim policing functions in line with the Strategic Guidance Framework for International Policing;

(b)	Support nationally led efforts to ensure accountability for crimes that fuel armed conflict, in particular those perpetrated against civilians, and crimes committed against United Nations personnel in peacekeeping operations and special political missions, strengthen prison security and management, re-establish the host State’s justice and corrections institutions and engage in trust-building initiatives;

(c)	Support the planning, design and evaluation of processes that remove weapons and individuals from armed groups and facilitate the reintegration of ex-combatants and elements associated with armed groups into society as civilians, and identify and formulate relevant lessons learned and guidance;
(d)	Undertake research and develop knowledge and guidance in the areas of rule of law and security sector reform and governance, and provide targeted, strategic country support, upon request;
(e)	Manage mine action programmes to mitigate explosive threats, train United Nations personnel and enhance national explosive ordnance disposal capacities;
(f)	Rapidly deploy specialist capacities in all the above-mentioned areas;

(g) 	Provide more coherent support to Member States through enhanced coordination with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs through the unified Peacebuilding and Peace Support Office, which consolidates the existing capacities of peacebuilding, disarmament, demobilization and reintegration, security sector reform, and justice and corrections expertise.
5.I.42	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	More effective and accountable security and justice institutions in countries receiving assistance, including increased accountability for crimes that fuel armed conflict and for crimes committed against United Nations personnel in peacekeeping operations and special political missions;
(b)	Reduced threats posed by armed groups and explosive hazards;
(c)	Sustainable peace and the prevention of armed conflict;
(d)	Mitigation of risks affecting the services of national and local rule of law and security institutions;

(e)	Continuity of United Nations operations in the areas of policing, justice, corrections, disarmament, demobilization and reintegration, security sector reform and governance, and mine action assistance.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened national and regional capacities to mitigate the threats posed by improvised explosive devices

5.I.43	The increasing use of improvised explosive devices by non-State actors continues to pose a significant threat to international peace and security. Recognizing the widespread devastation caused by these devices, the General Assembly, in its resolution 79/53, urged all States, as well the United Nations system and other relevant organizations and institutions supporting affected States, to increase attention to prevention and capacity-building and to provide support to reduce the risks posed by such devices. In 2025, the subprogramme, in partnership with subprogramme 4, supported 17 national and regional training centres across Africa and Asia in delivering training on the mitigation of threats posed by improvised explosive devices, in accordance with United Nations standards. To this end, the subprogramme assisted training centres with curriculum assessment and alignment with United Nations standards and trained and certified instructors across training centres, which are pre-conditions for the centres to obtain formal training recognition. Through the subprogramme’s support, four additional training centres gained formal recognition in 2025. The four newly recognized training centres subsequently delivered threat mitigation training to national security personnel from their own and neighbouring countries, thereby building sustainable national and regional capacities and enhancing preparedness to prevent and respond to threats related to improvised explosive devices.

children.text: Result 3: increased accountability for crimes committed against United Nations personnel serving in peacekeeping operations – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.I.49	In its resolution 2589 (2021), the Security Council called for specific measures to ensure accountability for crimes committed against United Nations peacekeepers. Since 1948, 1,081 personnel serving in United Nations peacekeeping operations have been killed and more than 3,100 injured as a result of malicious acts. Of these, 289 peacekeepers serving in MINUSCA, the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali, MONUSCO and UNIFIL have been killed since 2013, accounting for 84 per cent of the 345 fatalities attributable to malicious acts during the period. The activities of the subprogramme in support of the implementation of resolution 2589 (2021) have therefore focused on these missions. Significant progress has been made since 2018 to ensure accountability for such crimes, with an increase in the number of alleged perpetrators identified and detained, an increase in the percentage of reported cases of crimes against peacekeepers resulting in confirmed national investigations and, since 2020, 103 convictions for a range of different offences related to the killing of 35 peacekeepers and two United Nations experts in the Central African Republic, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Lebanon and Mali.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.I.50	The lesson for the subprogramme was the continued need to address both the political and operational aspects of supporting national authorities in their efforts to bring the perpetrators of such crimes to justice. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will prioritize the following six key initiatives: (a) political advocacy and information-sharing to strengthen engagement with Member States; (b) reinforcement of support capacities in missions to facilitate the provision of capacity-building and technical assistance to host States for the investigation and prosecution of such crimes; (c) further operationalization of the database on accountability for crimes against peacekeepers; (d) support for the Group of Friends to Promote Accountability for Crimes Against Peacekeepers; (e) public information activities to enhance visibility and support; and (f) reinforcement of capacities at Headquarters to provide dedicated support with regard to these efforts.

children.text: Subprogramme 4 – Policy, evaluation and training
Objective
5.I.53	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance the performance and effectiveness of peace operations within the purview of the Department.
Strategy
5.I.54	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Promote and facilitate policy development, evaluation, organizational learning, training and strategic foresight on issues relating to peacekeeping, taking into consideration the relevant actions and decisions contained in the Pact for the Future, including the review on the future of all forms of United Nations peace operations, as well as the Action for Peacekeeping initiative, its implementation strategy and the Strategy for the Digital Transformation of United Nations Peacekeeping, in line with the Data Strategy of the Secretary-General for Action by Everyone, Everywhere;

(b)	Support peacekeeping operations in the implementation of the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System, as part of the integrated peacekeeping performance and accountability framework;

(c)	Formulate, update and disseminate policies and practical guidance and provide support to missions in specific thematic areas, such as the protection of civilians, conflict-related sexual violence, community engagement with a view to supporting sustainable political solutions, and child protection, as well as the use of strategic communications, information integrity and the distribution of accurate content, and the countering of misinformation, disinformation and hate speech;

(d)	Strengthen knowledge-sharing and guidance development for peacekeeping operations and coordinate the development of guidance for the peace and security pillar and for the Department of Operational Support, in close cooperation with the Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs and the Department of Operational Support;

(e)	Build the capacity of policy and best practices officers and focal points in field missions through advice, coordination and training, and further strengthen systems and infrastructure for knowledge management by increasing the quality and number of relevant documents available to users in field missions;

(f)	Support Member States that are contributing uniformed personnel through strategic force generation engagement and expanded partnerships with national and regional training institutions and partners for the delivery of predeployment training and train-the-trainer courses and the timely completion and dissemination of core and specialist predeployment training materials to support predeployment training for troop- and police-contributing countries, specifically in areas related to emerging challenges;

(g)	Provide civilian predeployment training and leadership and management training for all mission components and support the delivery of in-mission training through train-the-trainer courses in operational and support skills for uniformed and civilian personnel;

(h)	Use a learning management system accessible by Member States to host courses, enable the registration of participants, deliver online elements of programmes and enable testing ahead of face-to-face course sessions.
5.I.55	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Improved and more responsive decision-making by senior mission management, utilizing data-based evidence from the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System, as part of the integrated peacekeeping performance and accountability framework;
(b)	Increased use by missions of specialized information, thematic policy support and guidance, reflecting lessons learned and promoting an exchange of good practices between missions;
(c)	Strengthened and sustained mission performance through consistently trained uniformed and civilian leaders, contingents and individuals.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened policy and guidance for protection of civilians in African Union-led peace support operations

5.I.56	Following the adoption by the African Union in 2023 of a policy on the protection of civilians and the development in 2024 of a joint African Union-United Nations road map to support implementation of Security Council resolution 2719 (2023), the subprogramme, in 2025, supported efforts to strengthen operational guidance for the protection of civilians in African Union-led peace support operations. This included providing support for the development of guidance documents and standardized templates for protection of civilians strategies and threat assessments for African Union-led peace support operations. These tools were subsequently applied in the review and update of the protection of civilians strategy of the Multinational Joint Task Force against Boko Haram.

Result 1: an online platform to foster direct collaboration among Member States on the preparation of peacekeeping personnel – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

5.I.58	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 52 Member States registering their peacekeeping-related training and capacity-building data in the light coordination mechanism online system, which exceeded the planned target of 35 Member States. The subprogramme’s work also contributed to the launch of the “Marketplace” feature in March 2025, with 48 offers and requests being published, which exceeded the planned target of 20.

Result 2: improved analysis and planning at the field office level to address local conflict dynamics and promote political solutions – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

5.I.60	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 28 per cent of peacekeeping personnel dealing with local conflicts utilizing the local conflict analysis and planning tool, which did not meet the planned target of 30 per cent. The target was not met due to resource constraints which prevented the expansion of field exercises to further enhance the use of the tool in all field offices operating in contexts affected by local conflict dynamics.

Result 3: strengthened prevention and response to conflict-related sexual violence in peacekeeping operations – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.I.62	Conflict-related sexual violence is recognized by the Security Council as a threat to international peace and security. United Nations peacekeeping operations play a critical role in preventing and responding to such violence, in line with Council resolutions and mission-specific mandates. Between 2023 and 2025, the subprogramme developed and conducted a global roll-out of a policy on conflict-related sexual violence and its associated handbook, which was jointly issued in collaboration with Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs, the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights and the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General on Sexual Violence in Conflict. The roll-out was accompanied by awareness-raising campaigns, training activities and in-mission support from the subprogramme. These efforts strengthened understanding of responsibilities related to conflict-related sexual violence across civilian, military and police components. They also established the foundation for a more structured, mission-wide and tool-driven approach to institutionalizing prevention and response to such violence in 2027 and beyond, as required.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.I.63	The lesson for the subprogramme was that while awareness-raising and training activities have improved understanding of the conflict-related sexual violence mandate, the absence of standardized, user-friendly operational tools has limited the ability of missions to translate policy requirements into consistent practice. The lack of clarity regarding focal point roles, high staff turnover and uneven leadership engagement have further constrained the institutionalization of approaches for the prevention of and response to conflict-related sexual violence across mission components. These challenges have been compounded by the evolving operational and resource environment in recent years, including reductions in mission footprints and in specialized capacity. This context underscores the importance of an institutionalized approach that enables sustained implementation of the conflict-related sexual violence mandate beyond dedicated functions. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will transition from ad hoc support to a structured, tool-driven approach to integrate prevention and response to conflict-related sexual violence across mission components. The subprogramme will roll out a toolkit designed to strengthen the integration of the conflict-related sexual violence mandate across civilian, military and police components. This is expected to strengthen institutional ownership of conflict-related sexual violence responsibilities, reduce reliance on individual expertise and support more coherent, consistent and results-oriented implementation of the conflict-related sexual violence mandate across peacekeeping missions.

text: II.	United Nations Truce Supervision Organization

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

5.II.1	The United Nations Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO) is mandated to assist the parties to the 1949 Armistice Agreements in the supervision of the application and observance of the terms of those Agreements. Its mandate derives from Security Council resolutions and decisions, including resolutions 50 (1948), 54 (1948) and 73 (1949). The military observers, assigned to assist the United Nations Mediator in supervising the truce in Palestine pursuant to resolution 50 (1948), became UNTSO pursuant to resolution 73 (1949) to supervise the Armistice Agreements between the parties to the Arab-Israeli conflict. Following the wars of 1956, 1967 and 1973, the functions of the observers were adapted in response to altered circumstances.

5.II.2	UNTSO activities in Egypt, Israel, Jordan, Lebanon and the Syrian Arab Republic are aimed at maintaining the regional liaison architecture established under the Armistice Agreements. UNTSO deploys trained military observers to the UNDOF area of operations to assist UNDOF in its mandate implementation and to southern Lebanon in accordance with the mandate of UNTSO.
Programme of work
Objective
5.II.3	The objective, to which UNTSO contributes, is to ensure adherence to the Armistice Agreements of 1949 and related agreements.
Strategy and external factors for 2027
5.II.4	To contribute to the objective, UNTSO will:

(a)	Deploy trained military observers to UNDOF to assist in its mandate implementation, and to southern Lebanon in accordance with the mandate of UNTSO, by providing timely and accurate observation, reports, investigations, inspections and verification. The mission provides administrative, logistical, communications and security support to all outstations and liaison offices;

(b)	Conduct periodic regional liaison through strategic engagement and regular interaction with the parties, as well as with senior representatives of troop-contributing countries and other Member States in the mandate area, and conduct regular liaison, through offices in Egypt, Lebanon, and the Syrian Arab Republic, and with Israel and Jordan from its headquarters;

(c)	Support informed strategic analysis and decision-making within the Organization, and with peacekeeping partners and other regional actors through regional assessments, and support peacekeeping partnerships in coordination with other United Nations entities;
(d)	Conduct seminars, briefings and information exchanges with regional stakeholders, think tanks and senior representatives of troop-contributing countries and other Member States.
5.II.5	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Reliable communications channels and relations of confidence with the parties, conflict resolution, reduced tensions, avoidance of incidents that could escalate tensions between the parties and expanded liaison and information networks with national and local community representatives and with actors with influence in the areas of interest;
(b)	Strengthened situational awareness and decision-making by other United Nations entities, enhancing the impact of regional United Nations presence;
(c)	Improved cooperation and understanding of cross-cutting regional issues among regional counterparts.
5.II.6	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:
(a)	All parties to the Armistice Agreements of 1949 and related agreements will cooperate with UNTSO in the performance of its functions;
(b)	All parties will remain willing to resolve their disputes and to cooperate with the United Nations in fulfilment of the Security Council mandate.

5.II.7	With regard to cooperation with other entities and inter-agency coordination and liaison, UNTSO will foster cooperation with a range of national, regional and international entities. It will engage with relevant national and regional organizations and collaborate closely with other United Nations regional peacekeeping operations, special political missions, humanitarian coordinators, security management systems and relevant agencies in all five countries where it operates. These engagements are intended to facilitate the sharing and validation of information from the ground, integrate assessments, ensure consistency in key United Nations messages and capture synergies to enhance the collective impact of United Nations presences in the region. Strategic cooperation will occur at multiple levels, through regular consultations and inter-mission engagements with heads of missions and agencies, as well as at the working level. In addition, UNTSO will expand its efforts to share information and analyse regional trends that affect the implementation of United Nations mandates, including through consultative inter-mission coordination activities.

5.II.8	The mission will continue to integrate a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. This will be strengthened through education and training for all military observers, as well as through the inclusion of female military observers in operations and female language assistants in UNTSO activities.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

5.II.9	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative.

5.II.10	UNTSO will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs.
Evaluation activities

5.II.11	The following evaluation completed in 2025 has guided the proposed programme plan for 2027: evaluation of the benefits and effectiveness of the UNTSO contribution to the increased operational requirement on the Bravo side.

5.II.12	In response to the results of the evaluation referenced above, UNTSO will focus on enhancing its support capabilities to effectively meet operational demands on the Bravo side. The mission will assess the vehicle fleet assigned to the Observer Group Golan–Damascus to support the strategic deployment of light armoured vehicles and is currently evaluating upgrades to observation equipment for its outstations. In addition, a project plan is being developed to improve living facilities for UNTSO military observers on the Bravo side. UNTSO will implement a comprehensive medical training programme tailored to the needs of the Observer Group Golan in Damascus, and review staffing requirements for language assistance to ensure adequate operational support.
5.II.13	An evaluation of UNTSO adaptation to the operational situation in southern Lebanon is planned for 2027.
Programme performance in 2025
Enhanced observation along the ceasefire line and in the area of separation

5.II.14	In 2025, the security environment in the UNDOF area of operations deteriorated as unauthorized military operations in the area increased. The expanded military presence and related activities, including the construction of military infrastructure, the establishment of roadblocks, and incursions into villages, affected the ability of UNTSO to record violations, which rose significantly compared with 2024. During the period, the mission reconstructed and operationalized observation post 52, enabling the mission to deploy additional military observers and maintain continuous and uninterrupted observation. This enhanced the mission’s ability to more efficiently document and report incidents and violations, while also improving communications within the area of separation along the ceasefire line (Alpha line), expanding coverage by approximately 25 km² and along 3 km of the Alpha line.

children.text: Result 3: strengthened planning and coordination mechanisms in southern Lebanon – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.II.20	In accordance with Security Council resolution 2790 (2025), UNIFIL will cease its operations on 31 December 2026, followed by its orderly and safe drawdown and withdrawal of its personnel by 31 December 2027. The withdrawal of UNIFIL is expected to result in adjustments to both the operational environment and the United Nations operational presence in southern Lebanon, including with regard to existing United Nations infrastructure and resources, in the context of continued implementation by UNTSO of its mandated activities in Lebanon and across Egypt, Israel, Jordan and the Syrian Arab Republic. In this regard, coordination between UNIFIL and UNTSO began in late 2025 to support an orderly transition. Maintaining transparent and structured dialogue with relevant stakeholders will remain important to monitor developments and ensure continued alignment with strategic guidance.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.II.21	The lesson for the mission was that early planning and coordination are essential to maintaining continuity in the implementation of its mandate in a changing operational environment. In applying the lesson, UNTSO will strengthen its planning and coordination mechanisms to support the continued implementation of its mandate in southern Lebanon.

text: III.	United Nations Military Observer Group in India and Pakistan

children.text: A.	Proposed programme plan for 2027 and programme performance in 2025
Overall orientation
Mandates and background

5.III.1	The United Nations Military Observer Group in India and Pakistan (UNMOGIP) is responsible for observing and reporting to the Secretary-General any developments pertaining to the observance of the ceasefire, pursuant to Security Council resolution 307 (1971). In the resolution, the Council demanded that a durable ceasefire and cessation of all hostilities in all areas of conflict be strictly observed and remain in effect until withdrawals take place, as soon as practicable, of all armed forces to their respective territories and to positions which fully respect the ceasefire line in Jammu and Kashmir supervised by UNMOGIP. To complement its ceasefire monitoring efforts, UNMOGIP conducts balanced investigations on any received complaints of alleged ceasefire violations filed by parties.
Programme of work
Objective

5.III.2	The objective, to which UNMOGIP contributes, is to ensure that developments pertaining to ceasefire violations along the line of control are monitored in accordance with the mandate of UNMOGIP as contained in Security Council resolution 307 (1971).
Strategy and external factors for 2027
5.III.3	To contribute to the objective, UNMOGIP will:
(a)	Continue to focus on the core mandated tasks of prevention, early warning, observation reporting and confidence-building;
(b)	Provide timely and detailed reports on relevant developments in its area of operations, in accordance with the implementation of its mandate;

(c)	Continue to deploy military observers on both sides of the line of control for the conduct of patrols, inspections and investigations of alleged violations of the ceasefire and the performance of other tasks in the vicinity of the line of control to the extent permitted by the host countries.
5.III.4	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in adherence to the ceasefire through the presence of United Nations military observers.
5.III.5	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Host countries cooperate with UNMOGIP in observing the ceasefire and refraining from hostilities and any action that may aggravate the situation or endanger international peace as embodied in Security Council resolution 307 (1971);
(b)	India and Pakistan remain willing to support UNMOGIP in the conduct of its activities for the delivery of its mandate.

5.III.6	The mission integrates a gender perspective into its operational activities, deliverables and results, as appropriate. This includes ensuring that the United Nations military observers engage in a comprehensive manner with local communities, among both men and women, to ensure that the mission has a gender-sensitive understanding of community perspectives of the situation on the ground and to facilitate the conduct and planning of mission operations.

5.III.7	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UNMOGIP will continue to collaborate with United Nations country teams in India and Pakistan for contextual insights on humanitarian and development matters, where relevant. This coordination is expected to enhance operational efficiency, including through streamlined reporting via shared platforms and reduced duplication in host country liaison.

5.III.8	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy, UNMOGIP will continue to consider accessibility needs, to the extent feasible within operational constraints, in its community engagement and reporting activities, to promote broader inclusion of affected populations.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

5.III.9	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects efforts of the programme to implement all mandates in a more efficient and effective manner and contains no substantive adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative.

5.III.10	UNMOGIP will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals.
Evaluation activities
5.III.11	An internal evaluation to be conducted by the mission, on a subject currently being determined, is planned for 2027.
Programme performance in 2025
Limited number of complaints concerning alleged ceasefire violations

5.III.12	To implement its mandate, UNMOGIP made every effort to monitor the ceasefire between India and Pakistan along the line of control. During 2025, the mission maintained supervision of the ceasefire through the continuous presence of United Nations military observers at its 10 field stations on both sides of the line of control. UNMOGIP continued to carry out its core field tasks, including patrols, inspections and investigations. The mission received eight complaints concerning alleged ceasefire violations during the year. In addition, UNMOGIP received 17 petitions from recognized political groups expressing concern over developments affecting the situation at and in the vicinity of the line of control in Jammu and Kashmir, on both the Indian and Pakistani sides.
5.III.13	Progress towards the objective is presented in the performance measure below (see figure 5.III.I).
Figure 5.III.I
Performance measure: number of investigated complaints (annual)
Planned results for 2027
Result 1: improved situational awareness through geospatial information systems – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

The target was not met due to implementation delays associated with required software adjustments to align operational maps with location-specific data.

Result 3: enhanced mutual understanding through strengthened liaison and engagement – Proposed programme plan for 2027

5.III.18	Persistent tensions and periodic ceasefire violations along the line of control continue to underscore the critical importance of reliable, neutral and timely information-sharing to help reduce the risk of miscalculation and contribute to stability for affected populations. Building on its established operational framework, UNMOGIP has consistently maintained liaison and engagement with host country military authorities, delivered briefings and updates to ambassadors and military attachés of troop-contributing countries as required, ensured public transparency through the maintenance of the mission’s website and delivered the full range of mandated substantive outputs.
Lessons learned and planned change

5.III.19	The lesson for the mission was that routine and structured information-sharing on ceasefire-related developments along the line of control, combined with systematic internal capacity-building – particularly through observer training which focuses on key skills such as consistent and accurate reporting, use of mission tools, patrol and liaison protocols and information-sharing practices – contributes to consistent, well-coordinated and professional conduct of liaison activities, which builds credibility and predictability. In applying the lesson, UNMOGIP will further systematize its established practices by prioritizing regular liaison engagements with host country military authorities and structured briefings to ambassadors and military attachés of troop-contributing countries as required. Concurrently, UNMOGIP will reduce reliance on unstructured outreach and place greater emphasis on consistent, standardized documentation and information-sharing to foster long-term mutual understanding, trust and awareness among relevant stakeholders.
